subject: LU-9618 clio: Use readahead for partial page write

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Patch Set 3:

Jinshan,

You commented you thought I could maybe remove the page list except functions.  I didn't see how to do that, did I miss something?

Patch Set 3:

Patrick, will this also trigger strided readahead in cases where the client is doing strided writes?

Patch Set 3:

My, readahead is complicated...

After some testing, I can say this:
No, it doesn't.  This appears to be because reads must be greater than 1 page in size to trigger strided readahead...  Possibly something to fix there.

A bit more on strided readahead:
The reads-required-to-activate/recognize aspect seems to make strided readahead tough for small i/o.  4k with jumps doesn't activate strided readahead at all.

8k with jumps (jumps must be large-ish or we stay in window for normal readahead, which is different...) activates strided readahead, but it misses a pretty high % of pages.  The same number of 1 MiB reads misses the same # of pages, so the issue seems to be detecting the pattern.

Anyway, single page reads don't trigger strided readahead.  I also suspect it only queues one stride out, so it's only going to readahead 1 page...  So...  Not much point, then.  If it queued multiple windows out, that would likely be interesting.

Patch Set 3: Code-Review-1

(2 comments)

Patch Set 3:

Going to reply to both comments here.

I see your point about using async so we don't have to wait for the rest of the readahead, but I'm not sure it can be made to work.  Also, remember this is only going to happen when we have a few writes in a row, so it's probably OK if one of them waits to make the others faster.

I don't think we can unlock the vm page in the middle of the write, that violates (at least) the assumption which the 'tiny writes' patch depends on.

And if we can't unlock the page, I don't see how we can do the async i/o.  I'm not getting anywhere, at least.

Patch Set 3:

Just putting this here before the weekend...

I'll prototype doing the write page in sync and the others in async, see if that nets any performance improvement.

Patch Set 3:

> I don't think we can unlock the vm page in the middle of the write,
 > that violates (at least) the assumption which the 'tiny writes'
 > patch depends on.
  
Definitely we can do it after write_begin() method is introduced.
Uploaded patch set 4.

Patch Set 4:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 4:

Ok, let's try this...

I did an A/B comparison here and I wasn't able to measure any performance difference from switching to async for the read pages (I still think it's the right thing to do).  The differences in the performance numbers in the commit are because I switched VM systems from last time...

Patch Set 4:

Code-wise looks good to me.

Patch Set 7:

Jinshan,

When I tried it, I could not trigger stride detection when doing partial page i/o (reads or writes, in fact).  But that may be moot soon given the LU-8964 patch, which changes the readahead algorithm.

Perhaps testing with that would be wise?

(Sorry I haven't answered Shadow's questions yet, I'll try to get to those, or perhaps Jinshan could)

Patch Set 7:

To be more explicit:  When I did i/o of less than a page, I could not trigger strided readahead.  I tried to figure out why and, frankly, gave up because that code is too confusing.

Patch Set 7:

Patrick - as long as it helps sequential read, I'll be fine to land it. I don't quite understand the original stride readahead algorithm either.
Patch Set 7: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 7:

This patch is not compatible with LU-8964 right now, since LU-8964 moves the readahead logic out of Lustre.

I am looking at that and trying to figure out how to connect ll_prepare_partial_page to readahead with the implementation in LU-8964.  I am not coming up with anything obvious - it seems like maybe we should call one of the kernel readahead functions (like page_cache_async_readahead or page_cache_sync_readahead), but I am not sure if they're the right choice.

I'll keep digging.

Patch Set 7:

Ok, I think I figured it out - page_cache_sync_readahead works well.  So when one of these patches lands, we should modify the other to work with it.

If LU-8964 lands first, I will update this patch for compatibility.  If this lands first, I will share the patch and maybe Dmitry will add it to LU-8964, otherwise I will push it as a new patch.

Patch Set 7:

Mmm, might be more to this, actually.  I will take some notes and try to deal with this later - With page_cache_sync_readahead, we are getting readahead in situations where I would rather not.  (Large unaligned i/o)

It still works OK for random, but for 16 MiB + 1K, we now do large readahead, which is *very* wasteful.  I'll see if I can figure something out...
Patch Set 7: Code-Review+2

parents.subject: LU-10463 osd-zfs: use 1MB RPC size by default

commit.subject: LU-9618 clio: Use readahead for partial page write
commit.message:
LU-9618 clio: Use readahead for partial page write

When writing to a region of a file less than file size
(either an existing file or a shared file with multiple
writers), writes of less than one page in size must first
read in that page.

This results in extremely poor performance. For random I/O,
there's no easy improvements available, but the sequential
case can benefit enormously by using readahead to bring in
those pages.

This patch connects ll_prepare_partial_page to the readahead
infrastructure.

This does not affect random I/O or large unaligned writes,
where readahead does not detect I/O.

Benchmarks are from a small VM system, files are NOT in
cache when rewriting.

Write numbers are in MB/s.

File per process:
    access             = file-per-process
    ordering in a file = sequential offsets
    ordering inter file= no tasks offsets
    clients            = 1 (1 per node)
    repetitions        = 1
    blocksize          = 1000 MiB
    aggregate filesize = 1000 MiB

New file (best case):
xfsize  ppr	write
1KiB	n/a	59.44
5KiB	n/a	164.5

Rewrite of existing file:
xfsize  ppr	re-write
1KiB	off	4.65
1KiB	on	48.40
5KiB	off	12.95
5KiB	on	143.3

Shared file writing:
	access             = single-shared-file
	ordering in a file = sequential offsets
	ordering inter file= no tasks offsets
	clients            = 4 (4 per node)
	repetitions        = 1
	blocksize          = 1000 MiB
        aggregate filesize = 4000 MiB

xfsize  ppr     write
1KiB	off	11.26
1KiB	on	58.72
5KiB	off	18.7
5KiB	on	127.3
